Name: adaptive_layerwise_quantization
Title: Adaptive Layerwise Quantization for Efficient Inference in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: Can we enhance the efficiency of neural networks in inference by introducing a layerwise adaptive quantization mechanism that dynamically adjusts precision based on real-time performance metrics and sensitivity analysis?
Related Work: While existing methods like SNIP and AMP-ViT address mixed precision quantization, they often apply static or heuristic-based approaches that do not fully utilize real-time performance feedback. Our proposal introduces a dynamic, layerwise quantization strategy that adapts to the input characteristics and operational context, enabling more efficient resource utilization without significant accuracy loss.
Abstract: In this research, we propose a novel Adaptive Layerwise Quantization (ALQ) framework designed to optimize inference efficiency in neural networks. Traditional quantization methods often employ a fixed or heuristically defined precision for all layers, which can lead to inefficiencies and performance bottlenecks. Our approach involves a real-time adaptive mechanism that adjusts the quantization bit-width per layer based on the sensitivity of the layer to quantization errors and the current operational conditions. By leveraging feedback from performance metrics, such as latency and resource utilization, we aim to achieve a superior balance between computational efficiency and model accuracy. We will validate our framework through empirical experiments on various neural network architectures, demonstrating that ALQ can significantly reduce inference latency while maintaining performance, thus paving the way for more efficient deployment in resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: 1. Baseline Comparison: Implement standard mixed precision quantization methods on benchmark neural networks and evaluate performance metrics such as accuracy, latency, and memory usage.

2. Adaptive Mechanism Testing: Introduce the ALQ framework, implementing the real-time feedback loop for layerwise quantization adjustment. Measure the changes in latency and accuracy during inference using varying input complexities.
3. Sensitivity Analysis: Conduct experiments to identify layer sensitivity to quantization through perturbation analysis, optimizing bit-width allocation based on results.
4. Real-World Scenario Testing: Deploy the ALQ framework in a simulated real-time inference scenario, comparing resource utilization and accuracy against static quantization methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the overhead introduced by the dynamic adjustment mechanism, which may not be suitable for all hardware environments. Limitations may arise in the form of increased complexity in implementation and the need for additional tuning to optimize performance across diverse architectures.
